concepts.name: HDD as Empirical Engine
concepts.definition: Christensen's most complete dataset: repeated architectural transitions in the hard disk drive industry where incumbents led sustaining improvements but entrants won disruptive form-factor transitions.

concepts.name: Architectural Generations
concepts.definition: Transitions such as 14-inch to 8-inch to 5.25-inch to 3.5-inch to 2.5-inch drives changed the value proposition, customer set, and application context.

concepts.name: Customer-Driven Resource Allocation
concepts.definition: Incumbents often had the technical capability to build smaller drives, but their best customers asked for capacity, reliability, and lower cost per megabyte rather than smaller size.

arguments.claim: The HDD pattern proves disruption is structural and repeatable.
arguments.evidence: Across multiple generations, incumbents performed well on sustaining innovation but missed smaller form-factor disruptions because the initial markets were unattractive to their customers and margins.
Incumbents could prototype new drives, but resource allocation killed or delayed them when existing customers rejected them.

cases.name: Seagate and 3.5-inch drives
cases.context: Seagate engineers built 3.5-inch prototypes while desktop customers wanted more capacity, not smaller drives.
cases.illustrates: Customer listening can suppress disruptive projects even when engineering can deliver them.
cases.lesson: Do not use mainstream-customer rejection as proof that a disruptive technology has no market.

cases.name: Smaller form-factor entrants
cases.context: Entrants served emerging applications such as minicomputers, PCs, laptops, and portable devices.
cases.illustrates: A small market can be the right home for an initially inferior technology.
cases.lesson: Find the foothold that values the new dimension.

logicChains.description: New form factor underperforms on capacity → mainstream customers reject it → incumbent shelves it → entrant sells to a smaller application → technology improves → mainstream demand catches up → incumbent is late.

terminology.definition: Physical drive size and architecture that shaped which applications could use the drive.
The rate at which a technology improves over time.
The rate at which customers can absorb improved performance.

nuance: The same managers that failed at disruption often excelled at sustaining innovation.
The data matters because it turns disruption from anecdote into a repeated pattern.
